meta.purpose: Define the axioms, ontology, and frameworks that govern test-driven development judgment.
meta.load_condition: Always loaded when this domain is active.

axioms.one_sentence: A test written after implementation tests the implementation, not the behavior.
axioms.full_statement: When the implementation exists first, the test is shaped by what the code does rather than what it should do. TDD forces the test to specify behavior independently of implementation.
axioms.why: Tests written after the fact confirm that the code does what it does, not that it does what it should.

axioms.one_sentence: Vertical slices produce reliable software; horizontal slices produce brittle tests.
axioms.full_statement: A vertical slice cuts through all integration layers end-to-end for one behavior. A horizontal slice tests one layer across all behaviors. Vertical slices are independently verifiable; horizontal slices create coupling across tests.
axioms.why: Horizontal slicing produces tests that break together when any layer changes, making the test suite fragile.

axioms.full_statement: If the test is failing (RED), the only valid action is to make it pass. Refactoring during RED either introduces unverified changes or delays the feedback loop.
axioms.why: Refactoring without a passing test baseline means any regression introduced by refactoring goes undetected.

axioms.one_sentence: A test that cannot fail is not a test.
axioms.full_statement: If there is no condition under which the test would produce a failing result, it does not verify anything. Every test must have a plausible failure mode.
axioms.why: Tests that cannot fail provide false confidence. They appear in coverage reports but verify nothing.

axioms.one_sentence: One test at a time. One behavior per test.
axioms.full_statement: Each RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle addresses one behavior. Writing multiple failing tests before implementing any of them creates indeterminate state and hides which test drove which implementation.
axioms.why: Multiple simultaneous RED tests make it unclear which implementation change fixed which test, destroying the diagnostic value of the TDD cycle.

axioms.one_sentence: The simplest implementation that passes is the correct implementation for this cycle.
axioms.full_statement: In TDD, the goal of the GREEN phase is to make the test pass as simply as possible, even if that means hardcoding. Complexity is added only when a subsequent test demands it.
axioms.why: Premature generalization during GREEN produces over-engineering. Each test should drive exactly the complexity it requires, no more.

ontology.one_sentence: Red-green-refactor is the cycle of writing a failing test, making it pass with minimal code, then cleaning up the implementation.
ontology.essence: RED proves the test can fail. GREEN proves the implementation satisfies the test. REFACTOR proves the implementation can be improved without changing behavior.
ontology.boundary: Red-green-refactor is not 'write all tests then implement.' It is an incremental cycle where each test drives one behavior.
ontology.trigger_signal: The agent is about to write implementation code without a failing test, or is about to refactor while tests are failing.

ontology.one_sentence: A vertical slice is a thin end-to-end cut through all integration layers that delivers one observable behavior.
ontology.essence: A vertical slice goes from the user-facing interface down to the data layer for one specific behavior. It is independently testable and independently deployable.
ontology.boundary: A vertical slice is not 'testing one function.' It is testing one behavior from entry point to persistence, including all layers in between.
ontology.trigger_signal: The agent is writing tests for one layer while other layers remain untested, or is testing multiple behaviors in one test.

ontology.one_sentence: A behavioral test specifies expected observable behavior, not implementation details.
ontology.essence: A behavioral test answers 'what should happen when X?' not 'how does the code achieve X?' It verifies output and side effects, not internal structure.
ontology.boundary: Behavioral tests can still be unit tests. A unit test is behavioral if it tests a unit's contract, not its internals.
ontology.trigger_signal: The test accesses private methods, internal state, or implementation-specific details.

ontology.one_sentence: A brittle test is a test that fails for reasons unrelated to the behavior it claims to verify.
ontology.essence: Brittle tests are coupled to implementation details: they break when the implementation changes even if behavior is preserved. They erode trust in the test suite.
ontology.boundary: A test that fails because behavior changed is not brittle — it's doing its job. A test that fails because implementation changed while behavior didn't is brittle.
ontology.trigger_signal: Tests break frequently during refactoring even though no behavior has changed.

ontology.one_sentence: A test seam is a point in the code where behavior can be observed or replaced without modifying production code.
ontology.essence: Seams allow tests to inject dependencies, observe outputs, or isolate units. They are the architectural prerequisite for testable code.
ontology.boundary: A seam is not a mock. A seam is the structural point where a mock can be inserted. Mocks are one way to use a seam.
ontology.trigger_signal: The agent cannot write a test without modifying production code, or the test requires complex setup that touches unrelated systems.

ontology.one_sentence: Hardcoded implementation is the simplest code that makes the current test pass, intentionally lacking generalization.
ontology.essence: Hardcoding during GREEN is not a shortcut — it is the correct TDD response when only one test case exists. Generalization is driven by the next test, not by anticipation.
ontology.boundary: Hardcoded implementation is only appropriate during the GREEN phase of the current cycle. It must be generalized when a subsequent test requires it.
ontology.trigger_signal: The agent is about to add complexity that no current test requires.

frameworks.name: TDD Cycle
frameworks.when_to_use: When implementing a new feature, fixing a bug with expected behavior, or preventing regressions.
steps: Plan the next vertical slice (one behavior end-to-end)
Write one failing test that specifies the expected behavior
Make the test pass with the simplest possible implementation
Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests GREEN

frameworks.name: Test Quality Check
frameworks.when_to_use: When evaluating whether a test is behavioral or brittle.
steps: Can this test fail? If not, it verifies nothing.
Does this test specify behavior (what) or implementation (how)?
If the implementation changes but behavior doesn't, will this test still pass?
Does this test test exactly one behavior?
Can this test run independently of other tests?

core_structure.via: Write failing test for each behavior → Implement minimally → Refactor with safety net

core_structure.from: Horizontal test suite (all tests for one layer)
core_structure.to: Vertical test suite (one test per slice end-to-end)
core_structure.via: Reorganize tests around behaviors, not layers; each test traces one behavior from entry to outcome

stances: One test at a time. One behavior per cycle.
If the simplest passing implementation is a hardcoded value, that's the right answer for this cycle.
A test that cannot fail provides false confidence.
